coredata.dc:title: Nano-biosensor based on manganese dioxide nanosheets and carbon dots for dual-mode determination of Staphylococcus aureus
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xue
Zhang, Hongmei
Liu, Lu
Jia, Mu
Li, Xuepeng
Li, Jianrong
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode nano-biosensor has been established for Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) determination. The prepared approaches of Manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) and carbon dots (BCDs) were facile, efficient and labor-saving and MnO2 NSs-mediated fluorescence quenching and oxidation could amplify detection signals. The dual-mode determination had a broad linear range of 37 ∼ 3.7 × 106 CFU/mL and low detection limits of 9 CFU/mL (ratiometric fluorescence) and 22 CFU/mL (colorimetry). Meanwhile, the method was applied in real samples with recovery ranging of 90 ∼ 102% and RSD < 4.44%, which was an insignificant difference with standard plate counting. The new dual-mode approach of S. aureus possesses the advantages of superior sensitivity, precision, accuracy and specificity. Moreover, the dual-mode nano-biosensor can be adopted in other foodborne pathogens determination by changing corresponding aptamers and provide an enlightenment in monitoring food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S. aureus
Nano-biosensor
Dual-mode
Aptamers
Magnetic separation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of MnO2 NSs-Apt1 and Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2
Application of dual-mode nano-biosensor in real food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM of MnO2 NSs, (B) DLS of MnO2 NSs, (C) Absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs and Fe3O4 NPs, insert: the photo image of MnO2 NSs (D) FT-IR spectrum of MnO2 NSs and Fe3O4 NPs, (E) HRTEM (left) and TEM (right) of BCDs, (F) absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectrum of BCDs, insert: the photo image of BCDs with visual light (orange) and ultraviolet light at 360 nm (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Optimization of parameters, (A) The F440 nm/F555 nm and (B) UV absorbance of diverse volumes of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2, The F440 nm/F555 nm (C) and UV absorbance (D) of diverse oxidation time.

(A) Fluorescence spectra for diverse concentrations of S. aureus (3.7 × 10 ∼ 106 CFU/mL), insert: the photographs of the mixture for diverse concentrations of S. aureus under 360 nm UV lamp, (B) standard curve of the F440 nm/F555 nm and logarithmic concentration of S. aureus, (C) UV spectra for diverse concentrations of S. aureus, insert: the photographs of the mixture for diverse concentrations of S. aureus under visible light (D) standard curve of the UV absorbance and logarithm of S. aureus concentration.

(A) Specificity evaluation for the F440 nm/F555 nm and UV absorption (B) in diverse bacteria with same concentration (106 CFU/mL) in PBS and the anti-interference in shrimp sample for the F440 nm/F555 nm (C) and UV absorption (D).
Schematic diagram of dual-mode biosensor for S. aureus determination.
Feasibility investigation in chicken, fish, mutton and shrimp adopting the fabricated biosensor and standard plate culture.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The spiked term refers to a food sample spiked with corresponding concentration. Detected results are presented as average ± standard deviation (triple measurements). RSD: relative standard deviation. P > 0.05 indicates no significant difference. Statistical test by Origin and SPSS.

head.ce:title: Nano-biosensor based on manganese dioxide nanosheets and carbon dots for dual-mode determination of Staphylococcus aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University, National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, Institute of Ocean Research, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology
National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage
Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products
Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province
The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University, National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, Institute of Ocean Research, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode nano-biosensor has been established for Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) determination. The prepared approaches of Manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) and carbon dots (BCDs) were facile, efficient and labor-saving and MnO2 NSs-mediated fluorescence quenching and oxidation could amplify detection signals. The dual-mode determination had a broad linear range of 37 ∼ 3.7 × 106 CFU/mL and low detection limits of 9 CFU/mL (ratiometric fluorescence) and 22 CFU/mL (colorimetry). Meanwhile, the method was applied in real samples with recovery ranging of 90 ∼ 102% and RSD < 4.44%, which was an insignificant difference with standard plate counting. The new dual-mode approach of S. aureus possesses the advantages of superior sensitivity, precision, accuracy and specificity. Moreover, the dual-mode nano-biosensor can be adopted in other foodborne pathogens determination by changing corresponding aptamers and provide an enlightenment in monitoring food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne diseases pose an immense burden on the global economy and food security, especially in low-income and medium-income countries (Pires et al., 2021). Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), a kind of pervasive Gram-positive foodborne pathogenic bacteria, is broadly distributed on account of its high robustness against external disturbance and low demand to nutrition (Hennekinne, De Buyser, & Dragacci, 2012). However, when transmitted via contaminated food and water, S. aureus can pose threats on human by producing a great number of toxins, including enterotoxins and invasive enzymes. Meanwhile, S. aureus contamination can infect human with various foodborne diseases, such as tetter, pneumonitis, septicemia, and infectious shock along with a high death rate (Gries, Rivas, Chen, & Lo, 2020; Karer et al., 2020). Up to now, food safety events related to S. aureus contamination have been in the third place (Potluri, Rajendran, Sunna, & Wang, 2020). Furthermore, Chinese food safety standard (GB 29921-2021) has stipulated that the limit of S. aureus in dairy products, meat products and frozen beverages is lower than 103 CFU/mL (“State Administration for Market Regulation, GB 29921-2021 National Food Safety Standard Limits of Pathogenic Bacteria in Prepackaged Foods,” 2021). Moreover, Australian food standard has stipulated that the limit of S. aureus in meat products is lower than 104 CFU/mL (“Food Standards Australia New Zealand, Standard 1.6.1 – Food Standards Australia New Zealand - Microbiological Limits for Foods,” 2016). Hence, there is an urgent need to develop speedy, responsive, selective, low-cost and labor-saving approaches for the early determination of S. aureus.

A variety of approaches have been developed in the determination of S. aureus, including conventional plate culture and rapid detection methods. Despite conventional methods are considered as the gold standard, they have the shortcomings of taking several days to produce results, labor-intensive procedures and a limited detection range (Eggers, Barker, Valentine, Hess, Duster, & Safdar, 2018). Quick approaches include nucleic acid-dependent ways and immunological-dependent ways. Nucleic acid-dependent ways, such as polymerase chain reaction amplification (PCR) and loop mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP), are time-efficient, sensitive and specific. However, they require well-trained personnel and expensive equipment (Galia, Ligozzi, Bertoncelli, & Mazzariol, 2019; Garg, Ahmad, & Kar, 2022). Immunological-dependent ways, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA), possess the superiorities of speed and sensitivity. However, they are susceptible to food metrics and need the professionals (Shang et al., 2019). To this end, it is extremely fundamental to developing the determination strategies with favorable selection, sensitivity, simplicity, stability and low cost.

Nanomaterials are environmentally friendly and well meet the needs of “green and efficient synthesis and sustainable utilization” (Jaleh et al., 2023; Raza et al., 2023b; Valian, Khoobi, & Salavati-Niasari, 2020), which are widely used in environmental governance (Raza et al., 2023a), biopharmaceutical (Valian, Khoobi, & Salavati-Niasari, 2020), disease prevention (Khoobi, Ghoreishi, & Behpour, 2014) and foodborne pathogens detection (Su et al., 2022), etc. However, the syntheses of some nanomaterials are complex, and they are only used for electrochemical single-mode detection (Hayat et al., 2023; Mosleh, Ghoreishi, Masoum, & Khoobi, 2018), which are affected by the environment interferences. Nanozymes, a kind of superior nanomaterials, exhibit more outstanding catalytic activity and higher stability than natural enzymes, which play a key role in promoting sensitivity of nano-biosensors (Ashrafi, Bytesnikova, Barek, Richtera, & Adam, 2021). Among various nanozymes, manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) have received intensive attention due to their nontoxic nature, ease of preparation, fluorescence quenching, oxidation and peroxidation properties (Cai et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021). The broad absorbance band of MnO2 NSs overlaps with the emission band of fluorescent nanomaterials, rendering them excellent fluorescent quenchers (Jiang et al., 2022; Liu, Lei, Zhang, Wang, & He, 2021; Zhang, Chen, Chen, Hu, Pan, & Hu, 2021). In addition, MnO2 NSs can oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethybenzidine (TMB) to bule oxTMB, and colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to yellow 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP) (H Zhang et al., 2021). Huang et al. employed the oxidation of MnO2 NSs to TMB for the direct detection of uric acid based on uric acid having an inhibitory effect on oxidation of MnO2 NSs (Huang, Qin, Xu, Cheng, Yang, & Wang, 2023). Hiremath et al. adopted MnO2 NSs-mediated fluorescence quenching for the “OFF-ON” fluorescent determination of Escherichia coli, further amplifying signals and improving detection sensitivity (Hiremath et al., 2021). Nonetheless, nano-biosensors based on MnO2 NSs have been rarely utilized in S. aureus detection.

Various modes of signals, including fluorescence (Wang, Feng, Xiao, Bai, Xu, & Xu, 2022), colorimetry (Yu et al., 2020), surface-enhanced raman scattering (SERS) (Wei, Haruna, Zhao, Li, & Chen, 2022), electrochemistry (Chen et al., 2022), have been used for S. aureus determination. Among these signals, fluorescent determination exhibits fast and sensitive merits (Wang, Feng, Xiao, Bai, Xu, & Xu, 2022) and colorimetric determination enables qualitative and quantitative analysis through naked eye observation and UV absorption, providing a simple and rapid method (Yu et al., 2020). Particularly, compared with single-emission fluorescence, the ratiometric fluorescence with dual-emission possesses inherent self-validation and eliminates environment and background interferences via the built-in reference signal (Wang et al., 2020). Versus single-mode determination, dual-mode determination possesses self-correction capabilities, reducing the occurrence of false positive and false negative and further enhancing detection reliability. Ouyang et al. developed fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode nanoprobes utilizing upconversion nanoparticles for S. aureus determination (Ouyang, Wang, Ahmad, Yang, & Chen, 2021). Similarly, Wu et al. established the similar dual-mode aptasensor based on multifunctional nanoparticles for Vibrio parahaemolytocus detection, providing further evidence of the satisfactory reliability and accuracy of dual-mode determination (Wu, 2019).

Therefore, this study established the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode nano-biosensor for the determination of S. aureus. The synthesis process was labor-saving, involving the simple ultrasound method for MnO2 NSs preparation and one step hydrothermal approach for blue emission carbon dots (BCDs) and ferriferrous oxide coated with amino (Fe3O4 NPs-NH2) preparation. Additionally, Fe3O4 NPs with magnetic property enabled fast magnetic separation S. aureus from complex food matrix. Moreover, MnO2 NSs-mediated ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode method for S. aureus determination was a new idea. The proposed dual-mode determination exhibited adequately five superiorities as follows: (1) MnO2 NSs acted a kind of prominent fluorescence quencher, having higher stability and catalytic activity than natural enzymes; (2) Blue emission carbon dots (BCDs) displayed superior fluorescence properties, high stability and good biocompatibility; (3) the magnetic separation step improved sensitivity via the quick separation of S. aureus from complicated food matrix; (4) the use of dual aptamers (MnO2 NSs-Apt1 and Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2) elevated specificity; (5) the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode minimized false positive and increased detection reliability.

Potassium permanganate (KMnO4, ≥99.0%, CAS: 7722-64-7) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), citric acid (CA, 99.5%, CAS: 77-92-9), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, ≥99.0%, CAS: 10025-77-1), o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 98.0%, CAS: 95-54-5), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES, 99.0%, CAS: 919-30-2), 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid (MES, 99.0%, CAS: 4432-31-9), N-(3-(diethylamino)-propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98.0%, CAS: 1892-57-5), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98.0%, CAS: 6066-82-6), etc. were purchased from and Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All of the utilized reagents in this work were of analytical grade unless otherwise stated. Ultrapure water (resistance value ≥18.2 MΩ·cm) was obtained from ultrapure water equipment (UPDROEMB-500L, Sichuan, China). The carboxyl group immobilized aptamer1 (5′-COOH-TCG GCA CGT CAG TAG CGC TCG CTG GTC ATC CCA CAG CTA CGT C-3′) and aptamer2 (5′-COOH- GCG CCC TCT CAC GTG GCACTC AGA GTG CCG GAA GTT CTG CGT TAT-3′) were fabricated by Bao Biotech Co., Ltd. (Dalian, China). One bag of PBS phosphate buffered powder were dissolved in 1 L of ultrapure water to prepare the phosphate buffered solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH = 7.4). The aptamer solution (10 μM) was obtained by dissolving in PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4) according to the instructions from Bao Biotech Co., Ltd. (Dalian, China).

The morphology and size assay of nanomaterials were observed in transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements were performed at wavenumbers ranging of 400 cm−1 ∼ 4000 cm−1 and recorded on a AKX1703426 FT-IR spectrometer. The magnetometer (SQUID-VSM, MPMS-3, Quantum Design Co., USA) was utilized to detect magnetic performances. The Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Co., UK) was applied to measure zeta potential. Fluorescence spectroscope was determined adopting fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi High-tech Co., Japan) at a 10 nm slit width. Absorbance spectroscopy was detected employing a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV-2550, Shimazu Co., Japan) ranging of 200 ∼ 800 nm.

MnO2 NSs were synthesized on the basis of the literature (Cai et al., 2022). In brief, 0.158 g of KMnO4 and 0.0195 g of MES were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water with stirring for a few minutes and sonicating for half an hour. Followed by the sediments were gathered, centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and rinsed with ultrapure water over and over again until the supernatant were achromatous and pellucid. Finally, the sediments were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water to obtain MnO2 NSs stock solution, and then MnO2 NSs stock solution was stockpiled at 4 ℃ for the next experiments.

MnO2 NSs-NH2 were synthesized based on the approach of Li Xue et al with slight changes (Xue et al., 2020), namely, 3 mL of diluted MnO2 NSs (MnO2 NSs stock solution and ultrapure water maintained the volume ratio of 1:4) were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and rinsed once with ethanol for removing redundant water. Then, sediments were resuspended in 3 mL of ethanol and 100 μL of APTES with reaction overnight. Upon washing thrice by the mixture of ethanol and ultrapure water (volume ratio of 1:1), the obtained MnO2 NSs-NH2 were stockpiled at 4 ℃ for further experiments.

BCDs were fabricated via hydrothermal reactor referring to preceding study (Li, Li, Chen, Deng, Ji, & Li, 2022). The mixture including citric acid (5.26 g), ethylenediamine (2.5 mL) and ultrapure water (50 mL) was transferred in an autoclave for 6 h at 180 ℃. Subsequently, the reaction solution was placed overnight. Then, the obtained BCDs were purified via suction filtration and stockpiled at 4 ℃.

Ferriferrous oxide coated with amino (Fe3O4 NPs-NH2) were synthesized in one step according to the method in literature with adjusting (Ouyang, Wang, Ahmad, Yang, & Chen, 2021), that was, the mixture containing 1, 6-hexandiamine (6.5 g), FeCl3·6H2O (1.0 g), anhydrous sodium acetate (2.0 g) and ethylene glycol (30 mL), was churned continuously at 50 ℃. When the solution became transparent and presented a uniform claret-red, it was moved into the autoclave for 6 h at 180 ℃. The reaction solution was rinsed with the mixture of ethanol and ultrapure water (volume ratio of 1:1) for at least 3 times and separated from the solution system via external magnetic force. The obtained Fe3O4 NPs-NH2 were dispersed in 40 mL of PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) for further process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of MnO2 NSs-Apt1 and Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 NPs were labeled by aptamer (Apt2) according to the method described in the literature (Sun et al., 2022) with modification. In brief, 300 μL of carboxyl group modified aptamer2 solution (10 μM), 100 μL of EDC (1 mg/mL, PBS as the solvent) and 100 μL of NHS (1 mg/mL, PBS as the solvent) were added to activate carboxyl group. The mixture was stirred for half an hour, followed by 1.5 mL of Fe3O4 NPs-NH2 were added. The solution was incubated overnight. Subsequently, an external magnet was used for separation and the Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 solution was rinsed with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) at least 3 times until no aptamer could be detected in the supernatant. Finally, Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 were dispersed in 2 mL of PBS buffered solution and stockpiled at 4 ℃. Additionally, MnO2 NSs-Apt1 were prepared with a similar method aside from centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min) as the substitution of magnetic separation.

The employed bacteria including S. aureus, E. coli O157:H7, L. monocytogens and V. parahaemolyticus were inoculated in L-B fluid medium respectively. 200 μL of activated strains were transferred to 10 mL of new fluid medium and incubated for 12 h at 37 °C with on-going swinging. After incubation for the third generation, the bacteria were continuously diluted with a gradient concentration of 10 in a sterile PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4), 200 μL of diluted bacteria solution was transferred to L-B agar plate and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Ultimately, the clump count of the diluted bacterial solution was measured by traditional plate counting approach.

Various consistencies of S. aureus were used to establish calibration curves in the range between 37 and 3.7 × 106 CFU/mL. The absorbance spectrum was measured in the band of 200 ∼ 800 nm, and fluorescence spectrum was measured with the excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Li, Li, Chen, Deng, Ji, & Li, 2022). The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the ratiometric fluorescence (F440 nm/F555 nm) and UV absorption against logarithmic concentration of S. aureus. The accuracy was expressed by the difference between the mean and the accepted true value together with the confidence intervals of 95%. The precision was evaluated by three repeated measurements on the same day (repeatability) and different days (intermediated precision), which was expressed by relative standard deviation (RSD). Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were calculated according to signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1 and 10:1, respectively. The accuracy, precision, LOD and LOQ were validated according to ICH Q2 (R2) guidelines (“International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use,” 2022).

For testing the practicability of the dual-mode biosensor, the dual-mode method was applied for S. aureus determination in real samples. The utilized chicken, fish, mutton and shrimp were purchased from the local market. 5 g of crushed samples and 20 mL of ethyl acetate were added in a centrifuge tube, then vortex extraction was performed for 10 min, solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min, and the extraction was repeated twice to collected the supernatant. After concentrating the solution, 5 mL of n-hexane were added with vortex for 30 s, solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min to discard n-hexane, the rest liquid was diluted with 15 mL of sterile PBS and stockpiled at 4 ℃ prior to use. When S. aureus solution in different concentrations (103, 104, 105 CFU/mL) were immitted, then tested according to the aforementioned dual-mode determination method and further verified by standard plate counting.

All data came from at least three repeated measurements. Significance analysis was conducted on SPSS and the data were calculated as the mean, standard deviation and relative standard deviation. Statistical test was performed on SPSS to compare the mean recovery of the dual-mode method and the standard counting plate with confidence interval of 95%. Statistical analysis was performed on Origin 2019b.

MnO2 NSs were essential for the generation of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-signal, characterized by TEM, UV–vis, DLS, FT-IR and zeta potential tests. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, the TEM image of as-prepared MnO2 NSs confirmed the representative 2D sheet-like nanostructure with occasional crinkles and folds (Cai et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021). Fig. 1C exhibited a broad UV–vis absorbance spectrum in the band of 200 ∼ 800 nm (curve a), and it displayed an emblematical absorption peak of the prepared MnO2 NSs at approximately 380 nm (Li, Li, Chen, Deng, Ji, & Li, 2022). Moreover, MnO2 NSs exhibited satisfactory dispersion in aqueous solution, and the mean diameter of MnO2 NSs was measured to be 257 nm (Fig. 1B), which was agree well with the previous characterization (Cai et al., 2022). In Fig. 1D (orange), the FT-IR absorption bands of the MnO2 NSs showed the characteristic peaks about 500 cm−1, corresponding to the Mn-O stretching vibration (Cai et al., 2022). The zeta potential of MnO2 NSs was −20.8 mv (Fig. S3 C), illustrating the surface of MnO2 NSs was negative charge. Above results indicated the successful fabrication of MnO2 NSs.

Fe3O4 NPs are known for their exceptional magnetization and ability to improve capture efficiency for bacteria. As depicted in Fig. S1A, The SEM image presented the morphological structure of as-prepared Fe3O4 NPs with a particle size of roughly 120 nm and a homogeneous dispersion (Ouyang, Wang, Ahmad, Yang, & Chen, 2021). The Fe3O4 NPs showed a broad UV–vis spectrum ranging from 200 nm to 800 nm in Fig. 1C (curve b). Fig. 1D (black) exhibited the diverse peaks at 1610, 1072 and 859 cm−1, mainly resulting from the shear curving vibrancy and rocking vibrancy of N–H bonds. The peak at 1378 cm−1 was given by –CH2- stretching vibrancy. Moreover, the high-intensity typical vibration peak of Fe3O4 NPs at 555 cm−1 associated with Fe–O bonds, confirming the successful formation of amino groups functionalized Fe3O4 NPs (Ouyang, Wang, Ahmad, Yang, & Chen, 2021). Fig. S1B illustrated the Fe3O4 NPs possessed benign paramagnetism with a saturation magnetization of 41.29 emu/g. Under the action of an external magnetic force, there was a shift from uniform distribution (inset a) to aggregation (inset b) of Fe3O4 NPs solution within 30 s. Above results confirmed the successful synthesis of Fe3O4 NPs.

The morphology and optical performance of BCDs were demonstrated via TEM images and UV–vis spectrum, fluorescence spectrum. As presented in Fig. 1E, the TEM images of the as-prepared BCDs exhibited a quasi-spherical shape and well-monodispersed. Furthermore, the high-definition TEM picture of BCDs showed lattice fringes occupying of 0.21 nm. Fig. 1F exhibited a remarkable peak centered at about 340 nm, associating with π-π* conversion of CC(X Liu et al., 2021). Simultaneously, Fig. 1F also indicated the fabricated BCDs owned a significant fluorescence peak at 440 nm and showed blue emission in a 365 nm ultraviolet light, which was an insignificant difference to the previously characterized results (Li, Li, Chen, Deng, Ji, & Li, 2022). Above information demonstrated the successful fabrication of BCDs.

For confirming the successful synthesis of MnO2 NSs-Apt1 and Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2, the UV–vis, zeta potential and TEM measurements were conducted. Before the attachment of the aptamers, both MnO2 NSs and Fe3O4 NPs displayed characteristic absorption peaks in their respective UV–Vis spectra. After attaching Apt1 to MnO2 NSs and Apt2 to Fe3O4 NPs, the UV–Vis spectra showed additional peaks at 260 nm, corresponding to the characteristic UV absorbance of the aptamers. This confirmed the successful preparation of MnO2 NSs-Apt1 and Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 (Fig. S2A and Fig. S2B). Fig. S2C revealed that the zeta-potential of MnO2 NSs changed from −20.8 mV to −28.2 mV, and zeta-potential of Fe3O4 NPs changed from −30.1 mV to −41.3 mV after the attachment with aptamers, which indicated aptamers had the negatively charged groups. Fig. S2C further confirmed the attachment of the aptamers.

Scheme 1 illustrated the mechanism of dual-mode biosensor utilizing MnO2 NSs and CDs for determination of S. aureus. With the rise of the concentration of S. aureus, the content of MnO2 NSs in the supernatant decreased after magnetic separation. Then, the fluorescence of BCDs at 440 nm increased and the fluorescence of DAP at 555 nm declined, with the yellow color of the mixture becoming lighter. Fig. S4 and Fig. S4B provided further insight into the feasibility of the dual-mode determination mechanism. Fig. S4A confirmed the ratiometric fluorescence determination. In BCDs + OPD solution (curve a), only the fluorescence of BCDs at 440 nm was observed. After the addition of MnO2 NSs (curve c), the fluorescence of BCDs at 440 nm decreased and DAP at 555 nm appeared. After the addition of S. aureus (curve b), the fluorescence of BCDs increased and the fluorescence of DAP decreased due to the decrease of MnO2 NSs content in the supernatant after magnetic absorption. Fig. S4B validated the colorimetry determination. In BCDs + OPD solution (curve c), no absorption peak was observed. However, in the presence of MnO2 NSs (curve a), the representative absorption peak of DAP at 420 nm appeared. In the presence of S. aureus (curve b), the absorption peak of DAP decreased, corresponding to the results of ratiometric fluorescence.

Based on previous researches, MnO2 NSs-mediated fluorescence quenching mechanism was mainly associated with inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Hiremath et al., 2021; Liu, Lei, Zhang, Wang, & He, 2021). In this vein, the UV–Vis absorbed spectrum of MnO2 NSs and fluorescent spectrum of BCDs were investigated. According to Fig. S4C. The wide absorbed spectrum of MnO2 NSs overlapped distinctly with both the excitation spectrum and the emission spectrum of BCDs, which indicated the feasibility of IFE and FRET (Hiremath et al., 2021). Moreover, according to Fig. S4D, the absorption spectra of BCDs remained similar in the presence of MnO2 NSs, confirming that no complex was formed between BCDs and MnO2 NSs. Additionally, Fig. S4E showed that the mean fluorescence lifetime of BCDs (13.92 ns) had an insignificantly difference with BCDs + MnO2 NSs (14.03 ns), illustrating the quenching mechanism of MnO2 NSs to BCDs was a static quenching process, and further eliminating the possibility of FRET. Therefore, the MnO2 NSs-triggered fluorescence quenching of BCDs originated from IFE. Meanwhile, MnO2 NSs, as nanozymes with oxidation performance, could oxidize colorless OPD without fluorescence to yellow DAP with fluorescence emission (Li, Li, Chen, Deng, Ji, & Li, 2022). This oxidation led to color changes and dual-emission fluorescence with diverse variations in the reaction mixture, enabling the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode determination.

To achieve higher sensitivity, the addition amount of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2, incubation time with S. aureus and the oxidation time for OPD were optimized. Firstly, the addition amount of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 was an essential parameter in the magnetic enrichment efficiency. In 1 mL of system, 300 μL of MnO2 NSs-Apt1, 200 μL of S. aureus and diverse addition amounts of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 (0, 50. 100, 150, 200, 250 μL) and PBS solution were incubated. The ratiometric fluorescence (F440 nm/F555 nm) and UV absorbance of the supernatant, after adding BCDs and OPD, were detected after magnetic absorption. As exhibited in Fig. 2 A, the F440 nm/F555 nm of the mixture increased with increasing the addition amount of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 till 150 μL, after which it started to decrease. Because 300 μL of MnO2 NSs-Apt1 and 150 μL of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 addition amounts could form the most sandwich complexes. However, when the addition amount of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 exceeded 150 μL, Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 could partly connected with MnO2 NSs-Apt1, leading to fewer sandwich complexes. Meanwhile, the UV absorbance of the mixture further confirmed that 300 μL of MnO2 NSs-Apt1 and 150 μL of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 addition amount had the best magnetic capture efficiency (Fig. 2B). Accordingly, 300 μL of MnO2 NSs-Apt1 and 150 μL of Fe3O4 NPs-Apt2 were the optimal addition amounts.

Secondly, the incubation time with S. aureus was another fundamental factor regarding sensitivity. According to Fig. S3A, the F440 nm/F555 nm of the mixture increased continuously as the incubation time with S. aureus was prolonged up to 30 min and kept relatively stable level with further incubation. Similarly, Fig. S3B indicated that the UV absorbance of the mixture gradually decreased until incubation time with S. aureus reached 30 min and then kept relatively constant. Therefore, 30 min was the optimal incubation time with S. aureus.

Thirdly, the oxidation time for the catalysis of OPD by MnO2 NSs can affect detection performance. In Fig. 2C and D, the F440 nm/F555 nm of the mixture decreased and UV absorbance of the mixture increased rapidly over time and kept gentle level after 16 min, indicating the oxidation reaction was full. Therefore, the optimal oxidation time was 16 min to achieve sensitive and fast determination.

Under the optimal analysis parameters, the ratiometric fluorescence (F440 nm/F555 nm) and colorimetry dual-mode nano-biosensor was utilized in the determination of S. aureus. In Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence of BCDs at 440 nm increased and the fluorescence of DAP at 555 nm decreased with the increasing concentration of S. aureus. Meanwhile, under a 360 nm UV lamp, the emission color of the mixture changed from yellow to blue. As shown in Fig. 3B, the ratiometric fluorescence (F440 nm/F555 nm) exhibited a favorable linear response to logarithmic concentration of S. aureus ranging from 37 to 3.7 × 106 CFU/mL. The linear regression equation was y = 0.19x + 0.62 with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9927, where y was the ratiometric fluorescence (F440 nm/F555 nm) of reaction mixture, x was the logarithmic concentration of S. aureus. The limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 9 CFU/mL (signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) was 30 CFU/mL (signal-to-noise ratio of 10:1). Similarly, in Fig. 3C, the absorption peak of DAP at 420 nm decreased with the increasing concentration of S. aureus, and under visible light, the yellow color of the mixture became lighter. The colorimetry detection also showed a good linear concentration ranging from 37 to 3.7 × 106 CFU/mL. The linear equation was y = −0.053x + 0.59, R2 = 0.9946 (Fig. 3D), where y was the UV absorption of reaction mixture at 420 nm, x was the logarithmic concentration of S. aureus. The LOD was 22 CFU/mL (signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1) and LOQ was 73 CFU/mL (signal-to-noise ratio of 10:1). Furthermore, Table S1 represented a comparison between the dual-mode method in this work and the recent approaches based on nano-biosensors for determination of S. aureus. The dual-mode method showed the advantages of wide linear ranges of determination, low limits of detection, unrestricted conditions. Moreover, the built-in reference signals between CDs and DAP in the dual-mode determination possessed self-correction capabilities, reducing the risk of false positive and false negative results caused by environment and background fluctuations, thereby enhancing detection sensitivity and accuracy.

Relative standard deviation (RSD) was utilized to verify the precision of dual-mode determination. Repeatability analysis was conducted by the S. aureus determination in chicken samples on the same day (intra-day). Intermediated precision analysis was conducted by the S. aureus determination in chicken samples within one week (inter-day). Moreover, the difference between the mean and the accepted true value was utilized to verify the accuracy. As shown in Table S2, for intra-day determination, the recovery ranged of 90% ∼ 101% and the RSD < 2.75%, for inter-day determination, the recovery ranged of 90%∼97% and the RSD < 4.44%, indicating the good precision and accuracy of the dual-mode method in S. aureus determination according to ICH guidelines (“International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use,” 2022).

The specificity of the dual-mode biosensor in detection S. aureus was validated via other foodborne pathogenic bacteria such as L. monocytogenes, E. coli O157:H7, V. parahaemolyticus and their mixtures with the concentration of 106 CFU/mL and the anti-interference in shrimp sample. As obvious in Fig. 4 A and B, S. aureus and the mixtures containing S. aureus presented higher value of F440 nm/F555 nm and lower UV absorption in comparison with other three non-specific bacteria, indicating that the dual-mode biosensor did not respond to non-specific bacteria. Similarly, the result in shrimp sample (Fig. 4C and D) confirmed that anti-interference in shrimp sample had a negligible influence on S. aureus detection. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode biosensor exhibited high specificity and anti-interference, which was due to the strong and specific binding between S. aureus and aptamers of S. aureus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of dual-mode nano-biosensor in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: In response to the practicability of the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode biosensor in real samples, the recoveries of S. aureus suspensions at reverse concentrations (3.7 × 103, 3.7 × 104, 3.7 × 105 CFU/mL) were measured in chicken, fish, mutton and shrimp samples, with the comparison with standard plate counting. As shown in Table 1 , in standard plate counting approach, the recovery range was 96 ∼ 105% and RSD < 4.97%. In the dual-mode approach, the recovery range was 90 ∼ 102% and RSD < 3.35%. These results demonstrated that there was an insignificant difference between the results obtained from the dual-mode method and the standard plate counting method (p > 0.05), illustrating the satisfactory applicability of the developed dual-mode approach in real sample determination.

In conclusion, the developed dual-mode nano-biosensor based on MnO2 NSs and CDs was successfully utilized in the determination of S. aureus. The synthesis approaches of MnO2 NSs and CDs were facile, efficient and labor-saving. The dual-mode detection possessed the advantages of broad linear concentration ranges of 37 ∼ 3.7 × 106 CFU/mL, low limits of detection, unrestricted conditions, good precision and high specificity under the optimal parameters. The results in the analysis of chicken, fish, mutton and shrimp were consistent with the standard plate counting method. The results confirmed that the proposed approach became an effective candidate for S. aureus detection, and opened up possibilities for the determination of other foodborne pathogens by changing the corresponding aptamers. Moreover, compared with single-mode determination, the dual-mode approach enhances detection reliability and minimizes the risk of false positive results, which provides an enlightenment in monitoring food safety.

Xue Gao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Hongmei Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Lu Liu: Data curation, Validation. Mu Jia: Data curation, Validation. Xuepeng Li: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jianrong Li: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137144.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
